speaker: interviewer
text: The date is November 27, 1995. The survivor is Ester Lebensold, maiden name Reich. The interviewer is Martha Frazer. The city is the Bronx. State, New York. Country, United States of America. In the English language. Today is November 27, 1995. My name is Martha Frazer. I'm interviewing Ester Lebensold. Please state and spell your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ester E-S-T-E-R L-E-B-E-N-S-O-L-D. Ester Lebensold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name-- maiden name is Reich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-A--

speaker: interviewer
text: R-E-I-C-H we have it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. I have to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Right. And what is your birth date and your current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is March 14, 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your current age? How old you are now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you please state and spell your city and country of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. My-- Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we spell it in English, L-O-D-Z.

speaker: interviewee
text: L-O-D-Z, Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe your life in Lodz prior to the Nazi invasion of 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a city, a normal city like every city. Went to school. And Lodz was a very active city. It was a lot of factories. The most was in Lodz. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did most people in Lodz work in these factories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they came out, they did very big comedians and writers and the-- very, very-- a lot of organizations, Zionist, Zionist organizations. It was very, very lively city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell us about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my family, like all families. We was a-- we got the little factory from [INAUDIBLE]. And my brother, he was the manager about it. And in about 14 years, we finished school. We start to work because everybody was very poor. You have to work. And the money we bringed-- we brought home, it helped. The parents with five children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were one of five children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Three brothers and me and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you the middle child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No I was the third.

speaker: interviewer
text: The third child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And my sister was the first. And we got the youngest, 17 years when the German killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of school did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was Poles and Jews together in school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the children get along, the Polish children and the Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very good. No. We try. We, we got no choice, so we have to try the best of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in the area that you lived? Was it mostly a Jewish area? Or was it mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I live-- we live in a beautiful section, in heart from the city. And I-- a day before the war, we were sitting in park-- it was a closer park. And we were sitting there, and a Polish girl passed through. And she says, what you doing here? You have to go to Pales-- to Palestine. In the last minute, the bombs was already falling on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea that there would be a war? Was there any thought that this could happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't-- we didn't believe. We didn't believe till Hitler sent out all Polish-- what there was born in Poland, all of them. They came to us. I don't know. They came to us, a lot of them. They emptied school for them, the synagogues, a lot of them. We thought is a propaganda. We couldn't believe. When we hear-- in that time, there was no television, no radio. And we hear the, the, the-- what-- the speech what is holding everything. And the 'Heil, Hitler,' it was hard to believe. That's why when we believe-- when we believe, a lot of people would be saved. We could run away from the beginning to Russia. No. It was hard to believe. It was from the millions.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were going to school, were you a member of any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I used belong to Betar, the Jabotinksys. Jabotinsky was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear him speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I saw him in person. He, he came, and we was waiting for him from the train when he came. It was now uniforms like the Hitlerjugend, brown and--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is what I read, that the Zionist youth wore the brown uniforms. It was supposed to symbolize the earth of Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We was wearing the brown. With the children, we was maybe eight, seven, eight years old. And we was waiting for him to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he spoke to the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what he said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was-- I don't remember. He, he was one from the biggest speakers, Jabotinsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he made an impression on people when he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the song we were singing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I-- in Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I have that in my-- oh, [INAUDIBLE]. He was against-- to give up. He was for war. He says, [NON-ENGLISH] you can take the one-- the country with soldiers and with ammunition. We're not going to give up. The song is in Jewish, but I'm-- explain to you-- is we're going to fight for our country. And we're not going to give up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he talking about fighting for Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: For Palestine, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: To have Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, (SINGING) [NON-ENGLISH] With ammunition, with soldier, you're going to get more. I can't sing because it reminds me everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes. Well, it's very good that you remember the song. It's interesting. So when--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] why we have to speak about freedom, to free our country. It's burdened with ammunition and soldiers and we ought to fight for it. We don't give up nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wanted everyone to go to Palestine--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And fight to have a country for the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's right, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, very Zion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to give up. Not like now, to make peace. He didn't believe in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you remain as a member of Betar until the-- until 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betar. Betar. Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in the organization until the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the-- till everything get finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you finished school-- you went up through seventh grade. And then you went to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how to explain. I was working in a [NON-ENGLISH], in a factory. The fac-- the machines, they give out-- the material, they came out with holes. And I got a special needle, and I fixed it and you would never see it was a damage.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, my finger is a little-- because I was always holding the needle in that one. You see? It's larger. Yes. Yes. So most of the young people, when they finished school, went to work in factories.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was working mostly. Yeah. Because it's very hard to get to college and to high school. And they didn't accept to college. A lot of people, they, they, they get the-- now how you call it-- they have to, to get Catholics, to change their religion to get in college.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they didn't accept the Jew-- too many Jews. They had to pretend to be Catholic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I tell a story from a friend of mine?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came from Russia. Her husband met her after the first war. He was a-- a Polish prisoner in Russia. And they came to Russia. Anyways, that's not important. Was very poor.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, he was a excellent tailor. Now he was gambling. When the-- when the war broke out in Warsaw, they were standing in the line for bread. She was tending and a little girl was standing. And a man said to her, would you like to go to Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was wearing a, a-- a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what [NON-ENGLISH], what the Christians was wearing. And she says, I can't afford because I'm very poor. He says, I will help you. She says, you don't know me, and all of a sudden you want to help me. And he says, you don't have to be scared from me. I am a doctor. I married a gentile, and I have to change my religion because I want to go college to be a doctor. I wasn't able to do when I'm a Jew. So I'm-- in my heart, I'm a Jew. And I'm going to help you. She says, I have nothing to sell. And he says, I'm going with my wife to, to Russia. Because you have to pay. The people, they came to pick you up to take you to Russia on the boat. And he says, I will send the people, and they will bring you to Russia. And you have-- she got a sewing machine, and she got a iron, things what her husband was using. He says, you have to bring a neighbor. It means you selling it, and they have to sign. And they have passed through Russia. They came alive with two children. His son finished engineering in Russia. And now they live in [INAUDIBLE]. She died, and he died because she was 90 years old. She died last winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war broke out, was there any thought in your family of trying to get to Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. Because the people, they sit for so many years, and my mother says, I have to give up this and I have to give up this. And I was working so hard for it. What is going to happen's going to happen. A lot of people didn't go-- give up nothing. Absolutely not. They could save a lot of lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first thing that you heard or you saw that let you know that the Germans were coming into Lodz? Did you see fighting? Did you see bombs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was-- in Warsaw, was a bit-- a big fighting. Not in Lodz. In Lodz, after a few days, they give up. No. The point is a lot of people from Lodz was running to Warsaw, but it was a propaganda-- it was a prop-- to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people run away, and they killed them on the way. It was a lot of people, and they got killed on the way to Ru-- to Pol-- to Warsaw. So as people we're trying to run away, the Germans would kill them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Run away. It was a lot of people, and they throw the bombs on them. That was the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and your family remained where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and what happened after the Germans came into Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They have to run-- they have run away from the city and to run to, to the ghetto. And after they make the ghetto there, when they came, they took all Jews. But they got-- they cut with the skin. It was terrible. It was terrible. They came to the hospital, all hospitals, they kill the, the crazy people. They took the babies'-- that's-- excuse me-- shit, and they throw on the wall. And I saw that. And I came home because my mother-in-law used to live-- they make bridges to walk because in the middle was a, a highway. The cars was driving, and we have to pass. It was bridges in Lodz. It was four bridges.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is after they made the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had four bridges connecting the two parts.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you walked across the bridges.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I walk, walk once across the bridges because my mother-in-law was there-- lived-- she lived there, and I was staying with her. And, and they didn't like it I was-- I was dressed-- maybe too nice. I was not allowed to speak Polish, and they took some water and they run on me on the bridge.

speaker: interviewer
text: They threw water on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they, they, they had pipes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, maybe let's go back a little bit. You mentioned your mother-in-law. Tell us about your marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: My marriage. I met my husband on a beach. He came from France, from Paris, to visit his mother. In the meantime, he was working on the beach, a safeguard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this before the-- before the invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they-- he fall in love with me. And he didn't want to go back to France. And we thought everything will be OK. We've made it. And when the time-- when the German came in, he run away to Russia. And after he came back, we married and went back to Russia. And the Russian soldier, they catch us on the border. And they keep us the whole night in like a ice box. And next day in the morning, they send us back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back into Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Poland. And my husband, he got pictures. And they was checking everything. If we have jewelry, we have money, they want to take us-- they want to take away from us. And he found a picture, my husband, in the same club with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who is this that was looking?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS man.

speaker: interviewer
text: A German SS man.

speaker: interviewee
text: SS man. Because he grow up in Frankfurt in Germany. After they moved to France because his sister married from the Singer factory, a son. And that's why they moved back to-- from Germany they moved to France. And in France, you can never get citizen papers. You can live your whole life, only when you have a son in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: So she went also to concentration camp. And she came from France to make for her daughter a birth certificate, to take out the birth certificate. Her daughter used to live in, in-- my nephew lives there now-- in Portugal.

speaker: interviewer
text: So his mother came from France and got caught--

speaker: interviewee
text: Came to make a pass-- take, take out-- to make a passport and a birth certificate for her daughter from Portugal. And he came to visit his mother, and he fall in love with me. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this SS man was looking and sees--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And after he went back to Russia-- because he left everything in Russia, his clothing, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: The SS man let him go because he saw his picture with him in the same club.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yes. And he says, good luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: To your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went back to Lodz, and he went back to Russia to-- to take everything and to come back. And that--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he never got back. And his name was Max?

speaker: interviewee
text: Max. I don't remember the second name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his mother, meanwhile, was in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was back in Russia, and did-- you never heard from him from Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one letter I receive from [INAUDIBLE] stamp because he hasn't got the address. You know [INAUDIBLE] stamp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So he disappeared into Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took him. He wants-- he-- they asked the people there if they want to go home or they want to remain in Russia. He says he want to go home. So if he says he want to go home, they send him to Siberia. Because it says the people, what they want to go back, and the Germans was there already. They are spies. And not, not one of them, they came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: They all went to Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you return to Lodz where your family was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I return to Lodz. Yes, my parents and my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: By that time, they were in the ghetto? The ghetto was formed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto wasn't closed in that time. If not, I wouldn't be able to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was everything from the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you came back, did you move back in with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I move with my, my mother-in-law because she was alone. And I stay with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the ghetto was formed, did you have to move from that apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I walk on the bridge to her and to my parents. I have to walk on the bridge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents had to move from their apartment into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did too?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got lucky. I got a friend, and he was, in the ghetto, a little-- a big shot. So he give us a nice apartment. So for my parents and-- because of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the apartment like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The apartment was a big room and a kitchen separate. And usually in Lodz, in the ghetto, apartment like we got was three families.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they got-- you got it just for your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the bathroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bathroom-- I, I don't remember. I think on, on, on outside. All of them in Lodz. Even if you live in the city in a beautiful section, all hotels. And the [NON-ENGLISH], the-- all of, everything, and it was the toilets always outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you first went into the ghetto, how did you get assigned to a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: With that man, what he give us the apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he got you a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the foreman there. Yeah. And he give-- he gave me the job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, this apartment he gave you was for your father and your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. For my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sister. Who else was living there? Who else in your family? Was your brother living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The oldest brother, he got separate because he was married. And they got a little boy, three years old. So they got [INAUDIBLE]. Not far, across the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what had happened to your younger brother by this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went-- my brother, what he died here four years ago, he was engaged from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a city they call Zawiercie, not far from Cracow. So he went to her. And they got married there. And he took my younger brother to stay over there with them. It was most cruel. Because Lodz, they count in right away-- they change for Lodz called Litzmannstadt. It means it belongs to the German. It wasn't Poland anymore, Lodz. It was Litzmannstadt. It means it belongs to the German. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a beautiful monument from [INAUDIBLE], and they cleaned up right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- your brother thought it would be safer to stay--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the-- in the small towns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So what happened to these two brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The younger brother got killed. They took him to work, and it was 25 boys, I think. And two boys, they ran out from the train to the forest. And after they count, it was supposed to be 25. It was only 23. And they killed the rest of them. And brother what he came out alive, my sister-in-law, she got from her grandparents, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a little land like a farm. And the Jewish people, they wasn't very good in farms. So she sold it to Polish people. And the Polish people was very poor. Every time when she came for money, and they says, oh, Hannah. I'm sorry we don't have now. Maybe next time. Next time. Anyways, when the German came to take them out to send them to Cracow, and she says, I'm not going to Cracow. I go the-- for-- to the farm what my grandmother gave me. And I want to die over there. And the baby was one year-- one year old. And the snow was so high. And the Polack, he likes so the, the, the town, even he wasn't able to work, and he came every day to look around. And he heard babies crying. So he, he went in that place where the baby was crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, oh, my god. What you doing here? She says, they throw us out from Zawiercie, and we have to go to Cracow. They want to send us to Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, oh, they kill all Jews in Cracow. I wouldn't like you should go there. And he says, wait a few minutes. I have to go home. And he ask his wife what he should do. He came back [INAUDIBLE] for them and for the-- for the baby. He took them. He got under the house. He got like a hole. He got the-- the German, they took away from them everything-- potatoes, what-- what's growing on the fields.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they cover everything what they got, a special hole. They didn't know from it, and they got over there the-- all the bread-- [INAUDIBLE] for they needed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he cleared out, and he let them. You put them in that hole. And after three days, he went to let them out. He knocked. And my brother didn't answer. Absolutely. He knocked a few times. And my brother says, what is going to happen is going to happen. We are not leaving. They was laying on the floor. They wasn't able to stand up because it was only for-- to put some-- And he says, and make this. You know what. And he says, what is going to be with us is going to be with them. And they were staying 27 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this hole.

speaker: interviewee
text: 27 months. And they give them a carrot every day. They give him-- he was poor. He hasn't got for himself. You know, he share what he got for them because he got the whole family [INAUDIBLE] and the daughters-in-law. All they live in the same house. So he share everything with them what he got. So he-- And after 7-- 27 months, he didn't know what to do, to let them out. Because he was scared maybe the, the Russian come. So he went to the-- to a Russian office-- to a Russian office-- general. And he says, I have to ask you something.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, what you have to ask me? And he says, what you doing with the Jews? He says, what you mean what I do with the Jews? I am a Jew, and I'm a general.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, well, I have to show you something. He took him by the hand, and he open the hole. And he took out my sister-in-law. She die on the way to the hospital. And my nephew, he was a year old, and he came out like that. He loves now in Portugal. Like that. And my brother, everything was teared from the rats. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So after he find out from my cousin they are alive, so he left the child in, in a home. And he came to Germany to look for his cousins. And they-- he stopped in Frankfurt, and, and Yom Kippur, he was in a synagogue. He met a friend of mine. What he find me after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my friend says, Jakob, I have a surprise for you. I found your two sisters. And they live in [INAUDIBLE]. I give them a home. My sister was in Bergen-Belsen, because she was-- a few weeks before she got to be married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is how he found the two of you and you found that you had a brother that survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Now he died here four years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: He died here in--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my niece, where she took me into the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.
